title: Python for Everyone #Post 03
excerpt: Now that you know variables and data types, it is time to teach your program how to make decisions. In this post, we learn if, elif, and else — the building blocks of logic in Python. By the end, your programs will be able to respond differently depending on what the user says or does.
tags: Python for Everyone a blog series
content:
Making Decisions in Python

In real life, you make decisions all the time. If it is raining, you take an umbrella. If you are hungry, you eat. If the traffic light is red, you stop.

Programs work the same way. We need a way to say: "If this condition is true, do this. Otherwise, do something else."

In Python, we do this with if, elif, and else statements.

Remember booleans from Post 2? True and False? This is exactly where they shine. Every condition in an if statement evaluates to either True or False.

Comparison Operators

Before we write if statements, we need to know how to compare things. Python has six comparison operators:

OperatorMeaningExampleResult
==Equal to5 == 5True
!=Not equal to5 != 3True
>Greater than7 > 3True
<Less than2 < 9True
>=Greater than or equal to5 >= 5True
<=Less than or equal to4 <= 6True

Let us try them in Python:

age = 20

print(age == 20)    # True
print(age == 18)    # False
print(age != 18)    # True
print(age > 18)     # True
print(age < 18)     # False
print(age >= 20)    # True
print(age <= 19)    # False

Common mistake! A single = sign STORES a value (assignment). A double == sign COMPARES two values. These are very different things!

For example:

x = 10       # This stores 10 in x
x == 10      # This checks if x equals 10 (returns True)

The if Statement

The simplest decision: if a condition is true, run some code.

if condition:
    # code to run if condition is True

Notice two very important things:

  • The line ends with a colon :

  • The code inside is indented (4 spaces or 1 Tab). Python uses indentation to know which code belongs to the if block.

Let us see a real example:

temperature = 35

if temperature > 30:
    print("It is hot outside!")
    print("Drink plenty of water.")

print("This line always runs.")

Output:

It is hot outside!
Drink plenty of water.
This line always runs.

Now change temperature = 20 and run again:

This line always runs.
When the condition is False, Python skips everything inside the if block and continues after it. The indented lines are only run when the condition is True.

The if...else Statement

What if you want to do something different when the condition is False? Use else.

if condition:
    # runs if condition is True
else:
    # runs if condition is False

Example:

age = 16

if age >= 18:
    print("You are allowed to vote.")
else:
    print("Sorry, you are too young to vote.")

Output:

Sorry, you are too young to vote.

Change age = 21 and the output becomes:

You are allowed to vote.
Think of if...else like a fork in the road. Your program always takes one path or the other. Never both, never neither.

The if...elif...else Statement

Sometimes you have more than two possibilities. That is where elif comes in. elif means "else if". You can have as many elif blocks as you need.

if condition_1:
    # runs if condition_1 is True
elif condition_2:
    # runs if condition_2 is True
elif condition_3:
    # runs if condition_3 is True
else:
    # runs if NONE of the above were True

A classic example — grading a score:

score = 72

if score >= 90:
    grade = "A"
elif score >= 80:
    grade = "B"
elif score >= 70:
    grade = "C"
elif score >= 60:
    grade = "D"
else:
    grade = "F"

print(f"Your score is {score}. Grade: {grade}")

Output:

Your score is 72. Grade: C
Python checks conditions from top to bottom and stops at the FIRST one that is True. Once a match is found, the rest of the elif and else blocks are skipped entirely.

Try changing score = 85 and notice how only the elif score >= 80 block runs, even though score >= 70 and score >= 60 are also technically true.

Logical Operators: and, or, not

Sometimes one condition is not enough. What if you want to check TWO things at once? Python gives us three logical operators:

OperatorMeaningExampleResult
andBoth must be Trueage > 18 and has_id == TrueTrue only if BOTH are True
orAt least one must be Trueis_admin or is_staffTrue if EITHER is True
notFlips True to Falsenot is_bannedTrue if is_banned is False

Using and

Both conditions must be True:

age = 20
has_ticket = True

if age >= 18 and has_ticket:
    print("Welcome! Enjoy the concert.")
else:
    print("Sorry, you cannot enter.")

Output:

Welcome! Enjoy the concert.

If either age < 18 OR has_ticket = False, the else block runs instead.

Using or

At least one condition must be True:

is_student = False
has_coupon = True

if is_student or has_coupon:
    print("You get a discount!")
else:
    print("Full price applies.")

Output:

You get a discount!

Using not

not reverses a boolean. True becomes False and False becomes True:

is_raining = False

if not is_raining:
    print("Great day for a walk!")
else:
    print("Better stay inside.")

Output:

Great day for a walk!
You can combine and, or, not in one line. When in doubt, use parentheses to make the logic clear: (age >= 18 and has_ticket) or is_vip

Nested if Statements

You can put an if statement inside another if statement. This is called nesting.

age = 25
account_active = True

if age >= 18:
    print("Age check passed.")
    if account_active:
        print("Account is active. Login successful!")
    else:
        print("Your account is inactive. Please contact support.")
else:
    print("You must be 18 or older.")

Output:

Age check passed.
Account is active. Login successful!
Nesting can get confusing very quickly. As a rule of thumb, try not to go more than 2 or 3 levels deep. If your code is getting very nested, there is usually a cleaner way to write it using and / or.

Checking Strings

Comparison operators also work on strings! Python compares them alphabetically.

name = "Alice"

if name == "Alice":
    print("Hello, Alice!")
else:
    print(f"Hello, stranger! I do not know you, {name}.")

You can also check if a word is inside a string using the in keyword:

sentence = "Python is awesome"

if "awesome" in sentence:
    print("Found it!")

if "boring" not in sentence:
    print("Agreed, Python is not boring.")

Output:

Found it!
Agreed, Python is not boring.
String comparison IS case-sensitive. "alice" == "Alice" is False. A good habit is to use .lower() on both sides when comparing user input: if user_input.lower() == "yes"

Putting It All Together: A Real Program

Let us build a login checker that uses everything we learned in this post.

# Simple Login Checker

CORRECT_USERNAME = "admin"
CORRECT_PASSWORD = "python123"

print("=== Login System ===")

username = input("Enter your username: ")
password = input("Enter your password: ")

if username == CORRECT_USERNAME and password == CORRECT_PASSWORD:
    print("\nLogin successful! Welcome, admin.")
elif username == CORRECT_USERNAME and password != CORRECT_PASSWORD:
    print("\nWrong password. Please try again.")
elif username != CORRECT_USERNAME and password == CORRECT_PASSWORD:
    print("\nUsername not found.")
else:
    print("\nBoth username and password are incorrect.")

Sample run 1 (correct login):

=== Login System ===
Enter your username: admin
Enter your password: python123

Login successful! Welcome, admin.

Sample run 2 (wrong password):

=== Login System ===
Enter your username: admin
Enter your password: wrongpass

Wrong password. Please try again.
Notice CORRECT_USERNAME and CORRECT_PASSWORD are written in ALL_CAPS. This is a Python convention for constants values that should not change during the program. It is just a naming convention, not enforced by Python, but it is good practice to follow it.

Key Concepts to Know Before Next Post

1. Indentation is NOT optional

Unlike most programming languages, Python uses indentation to define code blocks. If your indentation is wrong, Python will give you an IndentationError.

# Correct
if True:
    print("This is indented correctly")

# Wrong!
if True:
print("This will cause an IndentationError")  # No indent!

2. The pass Statement

Sometimes you want an if block that does nothing yet. An empty block causes an error, so use pass as a placeholder:

age = 15

if age >= 18:
    pass    # TODO: add login logic later
else:
    print("Access denied.")

3. Truthy and Falsy Values

In Python, not only True and False can be used as conditions. Most values have a "truthiness":

# These are all considered False (Falsy):
if 0:        pass    # 0 is Falsy
if "":       pass    # empty string is Falsy
if None:     pass    # None is Falsy

# These are all considered True (Truthy):
if 1:        print("Truthy")    # any non-zero number
if "hello":  print("Truthy")    # any non-empty string
if 99:       print("Truthy")
This means you can write if username: instead of if username != "" to check if a string is not empty. Both work, but the shorter form is more Pythonic.

See You in Post 4!
